narrative: I'm sorry to share that your law school application was rejected because our predictive model estimates a 45% chance you'll fail the bar exam on your first try, which is high enough to flag concerns for admission. Your profile shows solid Year 1 school decile ranking of 6, which is a bit above the typical student's 5.71, but your Year 3 decile dropped to 3, below the average of 5.53. Your LSAT score of 32 falls short of the dataset average of 36.7, and your undergraduate GPA of 3.0 is slightly under the typical 3.21. Coming from an upper-middle family income bracket, identifying as female, White, and planning to study part-time puts you in line with many applicants, though part-time is less common at just 7% of students. The model weighed these factors to predict your bar exam odds, ranking your Year 1 decile as the top influence, followed closely by Year 3 decile, then LSAT score. Family income, racial background, undergraduate GPA, study status, and gender came next in order of impact. Each of these pushed the prediction a bit toward expecting failure rather than a first-try pass, with your stronger Year 1 performance having the most sway despite the later dip. Compared to typical students, your below-average LSAT and Year 3 ranking stood out as bigger drags than your family background or demographics, which are more common and neutral. Overall, you're close to the edge—many similar students pass, but these gaps tipped the scale. To boost your future chances, consider retaking the LSAT to aim above 36.7, as that's a key driver you can directly improve. Focusing on full-time study instead of part-time could also help, since full-timers dominate the dataset. Building stronger late-stage academic performance, like in Year 3, through targeted prep might offset early weaknesses next time around. With some adjustments, you could shift that 45% failure risk lower and reapply stronger. Hang in there—many overcome these hurdles.
